title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption: Monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic follows a consistent seasonal cycle across decades, with values measured in million square kilometers on the vertical axis and months from January to December along the horizontal. Each line represents a decadal average — 1980s, 1990s, 2000s, 2010s, 2020s — plus a separate black line for 2025. All lines peak between February and March, then decline through summer, reaching their lowest point in September before rising again toward year-end. The 1980s line sits highest across all months, starting near 14.5 million km² in January and dipping to about 7.5 million km² in September. The 2020s line begins around 14 million km² in January and falls to roughly 4.5 million km² in September. The 2025 line starts below 13.5 million km² in January, peaks just above 14 million km² in March, then drops to approximately 4.5 million km² in September — matching the 2020s low but remaining below earlier decades through most of the year. From June onward, the spacing between decades widens, showing greater divergence in summer and early autumn than in winter. The gap between the 1980s and 2020s lines grows from under 1 million km² in January to over 3 million km² in September. The 2025 line tracks closest to the 2020s line across all months, never exceeding it and falling slightly below it in mid-year. The dominant pattern visible is a progressive downward shift in sea ice extent across decades, most pronounced during the summer minimum, with each successive decade’s line positioned lower than the one before it from June through October.
